steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — synchronized offbeats tend to give a song swing regardless of whether downbeats are delayed.: The researchers changed both downbeats and offbeats together, so there is no basis for crediting offbeat synchronization alone. ✗
  • Choice B — the acoustic properties that give a song swing are not easy for jazz musicians to manipulate.: The passage doesn't mention whether jazz musicians can easily manipulate the acoustic properties that produce swing, so there is no basis for this inference. ✗
  • Choice C — jazz songs that feature the piano are more likely to have swing than are jazz songs that do not feature the piano.: The study tested only piano solos and never compared them to non-piano songs. ✗
  • Choice D — the timing of downbeats and offbeats may play a crucial role in giving a song swing.: Modifying exactly that timing produced a more-than-sevenfold increase in swing perception, which is strong evidence that timing is a key factor. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: The experiment manipulated downbeat and offbeat timing and found a dramatic increase in perceived swing, so timing is the logical causal candidate. D is correct.
